source_metadata.title_raw: Dennis A. Mederos, Esq. - I-19-164 - December 11, 2019 - Tulare

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 11, 2019

Dennis A. Mederos, Esq.
Vice Mayor of the City of Tulare
415 E. Lois Ct.
Tulare, CA 93274

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-19-164

Dear Mr. Mederos:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other bodies of law, which may apply, such as common law conflict of interest, Section 1090, or rules of professional conduct.

Because your inquiry is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

Please bear in mind that nothing in this letter should be construed as an evaluation of any conduct which may already have taken place, and any conclusions drawn apply only to prospective actions. (Regulation 18329(b)(6)(A).)

QUESTION

Do Tulare City Council decisions relating to the Tulare Local Healthcare District ("TLHD") create a potential disqualifying conflict of interest for you given that your law office building is adjacent to the TLHD hospital campus?

CONCLUSION

Generally, you may have a potentially disqualifying interest in your law firm, law office, and clients. If the decisions have a foreseeable and material financial effect on your law firm under

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. 1-19-164
Page No. 2

Regulation 18702.1 or the firm's property under Regulation 18702.2, you may not take part in the
decisions. To the extent a prospective TLHD-related decision may implicate the closing of the
TLHD hospital campus as you suggested, the decisions may have a reasonably foreseeable material
effect on your law firm and the firm's property.³ However, this is a determination that can only be
made on a decision by decision basis. Generally, the analysis depends on the specific nature of each
decision including, but not limited to, the scope of the matter and the potential effects the decision
may have on surrounding businesses and properties.⁴ At this time, we can only recommend that you
seek additional advice when you have facts regarding a particular decision.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are an attorney as well as the Vice Mayor and Councilmember of the City of Tulare.
Prior to being sworn in as an elected official, you were very active in the community on various
issues related to the Tulare Local Healthcare District ("TLHD"). All of the legal representation you
and your law office provided TLHD the past three years has been on a volunteer basis. You
represent individuals involved in TLHD on personal matters that could potentially come before the
City Council.

You own various properties in the City of Tulare, one of which is your law office
immediately adjacent to TLHD hospital campus. Your building borders the hospital on both the
north and east sides.

Due to chapter 9 bankruptcy, TLHD requested a $9,000,000 loan from the City of Tulare.
You recused yourself from City Council decisions regarding this matter because of your volunteer
involvement with TLHD. The City granted the loan to TLHD, and you anticipate further City
Council issues regarding its implementation. In the absence of TLHD properly administrating the
loan, you are concerned that a closed TLHD hospital campus would have a substantial financial
effect on the value of your adjacent law office. You also generally inquire about the TLHD coming
before the City Council on other matters.

ANALYSIS

Under Section 87100, a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes,
participates in making, or uses his or her position to influence a governmental decision that has a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial
interests. Section 87103(a) and (d) provide that an official has a financial interest in "[a]ny business

³ The Act's conflict of interest provisions are triggered only by the interests enumerated in Section 87103.
Given your significant volunteer background on TLHD matters, you are generally concerned that your participation on
the City Council in TLHD matters appears improper. Generally, your volunteer activity does not constitute a
disqualifying interest in the THLD under the Act. You are disqualified from a decision under the Act only to the extent
there will be a reasonably foreseeable material effect on an interest under the Act, such as your business, property, or
clients. However, you may want to contact your city attorney regarding any other laws that may apply.

⁴ We also caution that you are prohibited from taking part in any decision with a reasonably foreseeable
material effect on any client of your law firm if you receive income from the client of $500 or more in the 12 months
prior to the decision. As you have not identified any client of your firm or a decision implicating a client, we are unable
to provide a conflict of interest analysis in respect to any client of your firm. If a decision implicates a client, you may
wish to seek additional advice identifying the client and decision at issue.

File No. I-19-164
Page No. 3

entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth two thousand dollars
($2,000) or more" or "is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management." Section 87103(b) provides that an official has a financial interest in "[a]ny real
property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two thousand dollars
($2,000) or more." Section 87103(c) provides that an official has a financial interest in "[a]ny
source of income... aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in value provided[,] promised
to, [or] received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is
made."

As a named partner in your law firm, who presumably is also receiving income from the
firm of $500 or more in any 12 month period, you have an interest in your firm as a business entity
and source of income. Furthermore, because you own real estate worth more than $2,000, you also
have a real property interest in your law office. Generally, you will be prohibited from taking part in
any decision with a reasonably foreseeable material effect on the firm or property.

Foreseeability and Materiality:

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest is presumed to
be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is explicitly involved in the decision as a named party or
subject of the decision. An interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the interest, including any decision affecting a real property interest as described in
Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).

Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to a decision's effect
on an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision. The regulation provides that
the decision's effect on such an interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." If the effect cannot be expected
absent extraordinary circumstances, it is not reasonably foreseeable.

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's interest is material. With regards to a business entity
interest, including a business entity that is a source of income, an effect is material if it is explicitly
involved in the decision. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(1) and 18702.3(a)(1).) Regulation 18702.1(a)(1)
also provides examples of decisions in which a business entity is explicitly involved.

For a business entity, including a business entity that is a source of income, not explicitly
involved in the decision, a foreseeable effect on the entity is material if the decision may result in an
increase or decrease of the entity's annual gross revenues or the value of the entity's assets or
liabilities in an amount equal to or more than $1,000,000 or five percent of the entity's annual gross
revenues and the increase or decrease is at least $10,000; the decision may cause the entity to incur
or avoid additional expenses or to reduce or eliminate expenses in an amount equal to or more than
$250,000 or one percent of the entity's annual gross revenues and the change in expenses is at least
$2,500, or; the official knows or has reason to know that the entity has an interest in real property
explicitly involved in the decision, or there is clear and convincing evidence the decision would
have a substantial effect on the property. (See Regulation 18702.1(a).)

File No. I-19-164
Page No. 4

The materiality standards for a foreseeable effect on real property is provided in Regulation
18702.2. Generally, an effect on real property explicitly involved in the decision is material if the
property is explicitly involved in the decision identified in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1) through (6).
However, to the extent that your property is not explicitly involved in a decision implicating the
TLHD hospital campus, the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a
parcel of real property is material whenever the governmental decision "[i]nvolves property located
500 feet or less from the property line of the parcel unless there is clear and convincing evidence
that the decision will not have any measurable impact on the official's property." (Regulation
18702.2(a)(7).)

You indicated that TLHD matters will come before the City Council for future decisions.
Although, you have not presented a current decision on the City Council's agenda involving the
TLHD. We cannot make a conflict of interest determination without a full description of the
decision in question and must analyze questions on a case-by-case basis. Accordingly, we are
providing general assistance to the applicable provisions of the Act to assist you in making the
determination in the future. Should you need additional assistance, we encourage you to seek
assistance from your City Attorney. To the extent you need additional assistance regarding a
specific decision before the TLHD, you should seek further advice identifying the decision and any
interests implicated by the decision.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Ryan O'Connor
Counsel, Legal Division

RPOC:aja
